# Solution Design: Config-Driven Runtime Options

## Purpose

Eliminate the manual command-layer wiring pattern that copies config
fields into options structs at every dispatch site. Replace it with a
type-system-enforced contract where every worker, runner, and review
path receives a single resolved configuration object that the type
system requires. Make the bug class — "config field exists but no
caller wires it through, so the loop silently uses defaults" — locally
unreachable.

## Scope

In scope:

- Architectural pattern: `ResolvedConfig` (immutable) + per-family
  `*Runtime` structs replace the `*Options` family today.
- Three production dispatch sites that must call `config.Load`:
  CLI `ddx work`, server `runWorker`, GraphQL `StartWorker`.
- Five options families that get migrated in stages:
  `ExecuteBeadLoopOptions`, `RunOptions`, `ExecuteBeadOptions`,
  `CompareOptions`, `QuorumOptions`.
- Three latent dead-config-field cases that surface during the
  refactor: `NoProgressCooldown`, `MaxNoChangesBeforeClose`, and
  `HeartbeatInterval` are hardcoded constants today; `ContextBudget`
  has no config home; `ExecutionsConfig.Mirror` is parsed but never
  consumed.
- Lint enforcement (Stage 4) that mechanically forbids the old
  `*Options`-with-durable-fields pattern.
- Test coverage uplift: the four disabled config tests
  (`*.disabled`) get evaluated and either restored against the new
  API or deleted with rationale.

Out of scope:

- The CONTRACT-003 boundary with `~/Projects/agent`. Investigation
  confirmed the agent module does not import `ddx/internal/config`
  and ddx pre-resolves at `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:104`
  and `cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:80`. No agent-repo changes
  are required and no contract document needs revision. Agent
  v0.9.9 (ADR-005 smart routing) further simplifies the boundary by
  removing `ServiceExecuteRequest.PreResolved` — `ResolveRoute` is
  now informational and `Execute` re-resolves on its own inputs.
  The ddx side just populates `EstimatedPromptTokens` and
  `RequiresTools` (new per-invocation hints) on `ServiceExecuteRequest`;
  routing decisions are owned upstream.
- Routing-layer cleanup driven by agent v0.9.9 (rendering composed
  inventory in `ddx agent route-status`, retiring `agent.routing.model_overrides`,
  dropping the "No model routes configured" fatal error path).
  Tracked separately as a small follow-up batch on top of FEAT-006;
  does not block this refactor and is not delivered by it.
- The wire shape of `agentlib.ExecuteRequest` /
  `agentlib.ExecuteResponse`. The refactor changes how ddx-side state
  is organized before the request is built; the request itself is
  unchanged.
- New CLI commands or operator workflows. This is a structural
  refactor; user-visible behavior is preserved.

## Problem

A reviewer recently caught that FEAT-022 Stage G shipped a
`ReviewMaxRetries` field on `*config.Config` plus a resolver, and an
`ExecuteBeadLoopOptions.ReviewMaxRetries` field that the loop honors,
but no caller in `cli/cmd/` ever read the resolved value and assigned
it to the options struct. The config field was dead in production. The
test that claimed to verify the loop "respects" the override actually
injected the field directly into options.

Investigation found this is systemic, not a one-off:

- 6 production sites that construct `ExecuteBeadLoopOptions`,
  `RunOptions`, or `ExecuteBeadOptions` across CLI and server.
  **Zero** of them call `config.Load` before constructing the options
  struct.
- Three dispatch entry points (CLI `ddx work` at
  `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1997`, server `runWorker` at
  `cli/internal/server/workers.go:878`, GraphQL `StartWorker` at
  `cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:77`) all skip
  config entirely and populate options from CLI flags or request
  fields only. Every config-derived knob across every options struct
  is dead in the same way `ReviewMaxRetries` was.
- Two more `ExecuteBeadLoopOptions` fields, `NoProgressCooldown` and
  `MaxNoChangesBeforeClose`, are hardcoded constants in
  `execute_bead_loop.go:292` with no config field at all. They have
  the same shape as `ReviewMaxRetries` would have had if no resolver
  existed.
- The Go type system permits constructing any `*Options` struct with
  zero-value defaults. The compiler cannot tell the implementer
  "you forgot to wire this field." Every new config knob is a new
  opportunity for the same bug.

The pattern accumulated weeks of cruft: each new config knob added a
field to `*Options`, a resolver to `*Config`, and silently relied on
every caller remembering to bridge them. The reviewer caught one
instance because Stage G's acceptance criteria explicitly demanded an
end-to-end YAML→loop test. Without that explicit gate, the gap stays
invisible.

## Approach

Three structural changes that compose:

### 1. `ResolvedConfig` is the only valid input.

A new immutable value type `config.ResolvedConfig` exposes accessors
for every durable knob a worker, runner, or review path consumes. The
type has no exported fields; clients call methods.

A naive "unexported fields" implementation does not actually prevent
construction — Go's zero-value rule means
`var rcfg config.ResolvedConfig` and `config.ResolvedConfig{}` are
both valid expressions in any package. The design closes this escape
hatch with a sealed-construction pattern: `ResolvedConfig` carries a
single unexported sentinel field set only by `Resolve`. Every public
accessor checks the sentinel on the first read and panics with a
specific message naming `LoadAndResolve` if the sentinel is unset.
A zero-value `ResolvedConfig` thus passes the type check but fails
loudly on any access — making the bypass unreachable in any code
path that actually consumes the value.

`ResolvedConfig` is shared across goroutines without synchronization
because no method mutates it. There is no `Set*` API, no public
mutator, no JSON unmarshal target.

### 2. Runtime structs hold runtime plumbing only.

`ExecuteBeadLoopOptions` becomes `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, with all
durable fields removed. The same applies to the four other options
families. A runtime struct holds:

- non-serializable plumbing (`Log io.Writer`, `EventSink`,
  `ProgressCh`, `PreClaimHook`)
- per-invocation runtime intent (`Once`, `LabelFilter`, `SessionID`,
  `WorkerID`)

Anything that could plausibly live in config — anything someone might
want to set persistently for a project — is forbidden from the runtime
struct. The compiler enforces this because the durable fields are
removed entirely.

Run, RunCompare, RunQuorum, ExecuteBead, and ReviewBead all change
signature to:

```go
func (...) Run(ctx context.Context, rcfg config.ResolvedConfig, runtime XRuntime) (...)
```

### 3. All three dispatch sites resolve once, hand off everywhere — atomically.

The three dispatch entry points (CLI `ddx work`, server `runWorker`,
GraphQL `StartWorker`) all gain a single shared resolution helper:

```go
rcfg, err := config.LoadAndResolve(projectRoot, overrides)
```

`overrides` carries CLI flag values or request fields. The helper
loads the project's `.ddx/config.yaml`, applies overrides as a layer,
and returns the immutable `ResolvedConfig`. Every downstream call
takes that single value.

**All three sites must be migrated together for the bug class to
actually close.** Migrating only the CLI site leaves `ddx work` from
a server context still bypassing config (which is what production
actually uses). Migrating only the server site leaves direct CLI
runs broken. The migration discipline (see §Migration) requires that
the loop migration be considered "complete" only when all three
dispatch paths route through `LoadAndResolve`.

There is no other supported way to obtain a `ResolvedConfig` in
production code. Adding a fourth dispatch entry (e.g. a future REST
endpoint that starts a worker) requires calling `LoadAndResolve` at
that site.

## Why this approach

Three alternatives considered and rejected:

**Alternative A: builder pattern on options struct.**
`agent.NewExecuteBeadLoopOptions(cfg).WithProject(p)`.
Surface looks cleaner but does not eliminate the bug class — the
builder still has setters per durable field, and a new config field
still requires updating the builder. Test code can still call
`builder.WithReviewMaxRetries(0)` to bypass real config. Does not
make the bypass impossible.

**Alternative B: documented immutability of `*Config` shared across
callers.** Cheaper to implement (no new type) but defeated by Go's
shallow-copy semantics on structs containing maps and pointers.
`*Config` carries `PersonaBindings map`, `*AgentConfig` (with
`Models map`), `*RoutingConfig` (multiple maps), and

367		Harness               string         `json:"harness"`
368		Surface               string         `json:"surface,omitempty"`          // catalog surface: embedded-openai, embedded-anthropic, codex, claude
369		RequestedRef          string         `json:"requested_ref,omitempty"`    // profile or model ref from the request
370		CanonicalTarget       string         `json:"canonical_target,omitempty"` // resolved catalog canonical target
371		ConcreteModel         string         `json:"concrete_model,omitempty"`   // concrete model string to pass to harness
372		SupportsEffort        bool           `json:"supports_effort"`
373		SupportsPermissions   bool           `json:"supports_permissions"`
374		State                 CandidateState `json:"state"`
375		Provider              string         `json:"provider,omitempty"`            // discovered provider endpoint name (e.g. vidar, bragi)
376		CostClass             string         `json:"cost_class,omitempty"`          // local, cheap, medium, expensive
377		IsSubscription        bool           `json:"is_subscription,omitempty"`     // fixed-subscription harness; preferred over pay-per-token within quota
378		EstimatedCostUSD      float64        `json:"estimated_cost_usd,omitempty"`  // -1 = unknown
379		HistoricalSuccessRate float64        `json:"historical_success_rate"`       // observed success rate from routing metrics; -1 when insufficient data (< 3 samples)
380		RejectReason          string         `json:"reject_reason,omitempty"`       // non-empty means rejected
381		DeprecationWarning    string         `json:"deprecation_warning,omitempty"` // non-empty when requested ref is deprecated
382		Score                 float64        `json:"score,omitempty"`
383		Viable                bool           `json:"viable"`
384	}
385	
386	// Default configuration values.
387	const (
388		DefaultHarness = "codex"
389		// DefaultTimeoutMS is the default idle (inactivity) timeout — resets on
390		// every stdout/stderr byte or agent event. 2 hours is long enough for any
391		// task where the provider streams progress; a stuck provider still needs
392		// DefaultWallClockMS to guarantee termination.
393		DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000 // 2 hours
394		// DefaultWallClockMS is the absolute wall-clock cap applied in addition to
395		// the idle timeout. It fires regardless of stream/event activity, so a
396		// provider that emits heartbeats forever cannot pin a worker indefinitely.
397		// Sized at 3h — roughly 1.5x the idle timeout — so normal long tasks still
398		// complete while genuinely hung workers free themselves. See
399		// RC2 of ddx-0a651925 for the incident that motivated this bound.
400		DefaultWallClockMS = 10800000 // 3 hours
401		DefaultLogDir      = ".ddx/agent-logs"
402	)
403	
404	// ResolveLogDir returns an absolute session-log directory path anchored at
405	// projectRoot. Callers that construct an agent.Runner must use this to avoid
406	// the Runner's relative DefaultLogDir resolving against process CWD — which
407	// historically wrote logs to stray locations like cli/internal/server/.ddx/.
408	func ResolveLogDir(projectRoot, configured string) string {
409		if configured == "" {
410			configured = DefaultLogDir
411		}
412		if filepath.IsAbs(configured) {
413			return configured
414		}
415		if projectRoot == "" {
416			return configured
417		}
418		return filepath.Join(projectRoot, configured)
419	}

5	// TestLoopConfigOpts names every durable knob the execute-bead loop
6	// reads via ResolvedConfig. Tests must specify each field explicitly;
7	// there are no zero-value defaults that silently bypass real config.
8	//
9	// See SD-024 / TD-024 §Test config constructors.
10	type TestLoopConfigOpts struct {
11		Assignee                string
12		ReviewMaxRetries        int
13		NoProgressCooldown      time.Duration
14		MaxNoChangesBeforeClose int
15		HeartbeatInterval       time.Duration
16		Harness                 string
17		Model                   string
18		Profile                 string
19		MinTier                 string
20		MaxTier                 string
21		EvidenceCaps            EvidenceCapsConfig
22	}
23	
24	// NewTestConfigForLoop returns a *Config that, when Resolve()d with the
25	// matching CLIOverrides, produces a ResolvedConfig whose loop-relevant
26	// accessors return exactly the values supplied in opts.
27	//
28	// Tests must obtain ResolvedConfig via Resolve on the returned *Config
29	// — there is no shortcut to construct a ResolvedConfig directly. Pure
30	// CLI-override fields (Assignee, Profile, MinTier, MaxTier) are passed
31	// through CLIOverrides at the call site; the constructor stores every
32	// other field on *Config so it round-trips through Resolve.
33	func NewTestConfigForLoop(opts TestLoopConfigOpts) *Config {
34		reviewMaxRetries := opts.ReviewMaxRetries
35		maxNoChanges := opts.MaxNoChangesBeforeClose
36	
37		caps := opts.EvidenceCaps
38	
39		return &Config{
40			Version: "1.0",
41			Agent: &AgentConfig{
42				Harness: opts.Harness,
43				Model:   opts.Model,
44			},
45			Workers: &WorkersConfig{
46				NoProgressCooldown:      opts.NoProgressCooldown.String(),
47				MaxNoChangesBeforeClose: &maxNoChanges,
48				HeartbeatInterval:       opts.HeartbeatInterval.String(),
49			},
50			ReviewMaxRetries: &reviewMaxRetries,
51			EvidenceCaps:     &caps,
52		}
53	}
54	
55	// TestRunConfigOpts names every durable knob the agent run path reads
56	// via ResolvedConfig (per SD-024 §RunOptions → AgentRunRuntime field
57	// classification). Tests must specify each field explicitly; there are
58	// no zero-value defaults that silently bypass real config.
59	//
60	// See SD-024 / TD-024 §Test config constructors and §Stage 2 bead 17.
61	type TestRunConfigOpts struct {
62		Harness       string
63		Model         string
64		Provider      string
65		ModelRef      string
66		Effort        string
67		Permissions   string
68		Timeout       time.Duration
69		WallClock     time.Duration
70		SessionLogDir string
71	}
72	
73	// NewTestConfigForRun returns a *Config that, when Resolve()d with the
74	// matching CLIOverrides, produces a ResolvedConfig whose run-path
75	// accessors return the values supplied in opts. Pure CLI-override
76	// fields (Provider, ModelRef, Effort) have no durable home on
77	// AgentConfig and must be applied at Resolve time via CLIOverrides.
78	func NewTestConfigForRun(opts TestRunConfigOpts) *Config {
79		return &Config{
80			Version: "1.0",
81			Agent: &AgentConfig{
82				Harness:       opts.Harness,
83				Model:         opts.Model,
84				TimeoutMS:     int(opts.Timeout / time.Millisecond),
85				SessionLogDir: opts.SessionLogDir,
86				Permissions:   opts.Permissions,
87			},
88		}
89	}
90	
91	// TestLoopOverrides returns the CLIOverrides that, combined with the
92	// *Config produced by NewTestConfigForLoop(opts), drive a Resolve call
93	// to a ResolvedConfig matching opts. Pure-override fields (Assignee,
94	// Profile, MinTier, MaxTier) have no durable home on *Config; they are
95	// applied at Resolve time only.
96	func TestLoopOverrides(opts TestLoopConfigOpts) CLIOverrides {
97		return CLIOverrides{
98			Assignee: opts.Assignee,
99			Profile:  opts.Profile,
100			MinTier:  opts.MinTier,
101			MaxTier:  opts.MaxTier,
102		}

14	// SD-024 Stage 2 / bead ddx-4c31e465 behavioral test.

17	// flag values; agent.* fields in .ddx/config.yaml were dead at the
18	// compare dispatch site. With the migration, LoadAndResolve resolves
19	// the durable knobs (Model, Effort, Permissions, Timeout) into rcfg
20	// and CompareRuntime carries only per-invocation intent.

23	// config file from disk, dispatches a two-arm virtual compare, and

25	// the configured virtual response. This proves the new code path
26	// executes successfully against a real ResolvedConfig.
27	func TestAgentCompareDispatchUsesConfigResolve(t *testing.T) {

29		t.Setenv("DDX_VIRTUAL_RESPONSES",
30			`[{"prompt_match":"hello","response":"compare-arm-response"}]`)
31	
32		dir := agentTestDirWithHarness(t, "virtual")
33		rootCmd := NewCommandFactory(dir).NewRootCommand()
34		output, err := executeCommand(rootCmd, "agent", "run",
35			"--compare",
36			"--harnesses", "virtual,virtual",
37			"--text", "hello",
38			"--json",
39		)
40		require.NoError(t, err, "compare dispatch via LoadAndResolve must succeed")
41	
42		var record struct {
43			ID   string `json:"id"`
44			Arms []struct {
45				Harness  string `json:"harness"`
46				Output   string `json:"output"`
47				ExitCode int    `json:"exit_code"`
48			} `json:"arms"`
49		}
50		require.NoError(t, json.Unmarshal([]byte(output), &record))
51		require.Len(t, record.Arms, 2, "both compare arms must dispatch through migrated path")
52		for i, arm := range record.Arms {
53			assert.Equal(t, 0, arm.ExitCode, "arm %d should succeed under rcfg-driven dispatch", i)
54			assert.Equal(t, "compare-arm-response", arm.Output, "arm %d should receive virtual response via migrated dispatch", i)
55		}
56	}

13	// --harness flag, the agent.harness value from .ddx/config.yaml drives
14	// the run. SD-024 Stage 2 / Bead ddx-c0ff5d3e behavioral test.
15	//
16	// Before the migration, agent_cmd.go built RunOptions directly from
17	// flags and the harness defaulted to "" (service-routed) when the user
18	// omitted --harness — config's agent.harness was dead. With the
19	// migration, LoadAndResolve resolves agent.harness into rcfg.Harness()
20	// and AgentRunRuntime carries only per-invocation intent, so the
21	// configured harness reaches RunViaService.
22	func TestAgentRunDispatchUsesConfigHarness(t *testing.T) {
23		t.Setenv("DDX_DISABLE_UPDATE_CHECK", "1")
24		t.Setenv("DDX_VIRTUAL_RESPONSES",
25			`[{"prompt_match":"hello","response":"config-driven virtual response"}]`)
26	
27		// Config sets agent.harness: virtual; the test invokes `ddx agent run`
28		// with NO --harness flag. The only way the virtual harness can be
29		// selected is via config.LoadAndResolve threading agent.harness into
30		// rcfg.Harness().
31		dir := agentTestDirWithHarness(t, "virtual")
32		rootCmd := NewCommandFactory(dir).NewRootCommand()
33		output, err := executeCommand(rootCmd, "agent", "run",
34			"--text", "hello",
35		)
36		require.NoError(t, err, "config-resolved harness must drive the run path")
37		assert.Equal(t, "config-driven virtual response", strings.TrimSpace(output),
38			"agent.harness from .ddx/config.yaml must drive the run when --harness is omitted")
39	}
